answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent injury or activity that might have caused the pain?
answers.intake_a3: no, but the pain started when I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q6: Any difficulty moving your elbow or pain when you try to bend or straighten it?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a7: I iced the elbow the first day and it helped a little with the pain
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms since the pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any clicking, popping, or locking sensations in your elbow?
answers.intake_summary: A patient presents with 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain that began during tennis without injury, no swelling, redness, systemic symptoms, or neurological deficits. The pain does not limit range of motion and partially improved with ice.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her right elbow. The pain began during tennis without any specific injury. She reports no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or limitation in elbow movement. Ice applied on the first day provided some relief. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills, and no clicking, popping, or locking sensations have been noted. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain triggered by repetitive wrist extension activities such as tennis. There is no evidence of infection, neurological compromise, or joint instability.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen at recommended doses to reduce pain and inflammation. 
- Alternatives: Topical NSAID gels applied over the lateral elbow to minimize systemic side effects. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, cardiovascular, renal, or hepatic conditions. Avoid exceeding recommended doses. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek further care if pain worsens, if there is new swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, weakness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical presentation and absence of red flags. Consider imaging or nerve conduction studies if symptoms persist or worsen despite conservative management.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…
